summary:
Infections can lead to various complications; however, among the provided choices for fetal congenital CMV infection, \textbf{\underline{colitis}} stands out because although directly stated evidence may be lacking, indirect support comes via potential gut involvement related to broader inflammatory responses triggered by this specific pathogen during pregnancy. This makes option \(A\) more plausible compared to other less frequently reported issues such as heart problems (\(B\)), abnormal blood cell counts (\(C\)) ,or lung anomalies(\(D\)). To reiterate succinctly within two sentences focusing strictly on critical aspects pertinent here –
Congenitally affected infants often exhibit signs including neurological deficits & sensorineural deafness yet GI tract disturbances remain significant albeit non-specific findings warranting consideration especially when suspecting active maternal-fetal transmission dynamics involving CMV exposure early gestation stages potentially impacting multiple organ systems indirectly through immune response mechanisms rather than primary tissue damage alone. Given limited data supporting each condition specifically linked back solely towards CMV itself without additional context clues pointing elsewhere suggests prioritizing those most likely based purely off general knowledge about how viruses interact broadly across human physiology post-exposure irrespective of exact mechanism details underlying individual case presentations variability inherent even amongst similar pathogens sharing broad biological traits influencing outcomes differently depending upon host factors involved at time point of initial contact making colitis stand out despite lackluster direct proof existing currently against all others presented herein being ruled definitively absent clinically speaking under normal circumstances encountered routinely throughout obstetric
